summary: Julia Evans discovered a practical use for the ed text editor when she needed to batch edit 200 files at work. After reading Michael W Lucas's 'Ed Mastery' book (published as a semi-joke on April Fools' Day), she learned that any ed session can be replayed as a script, making it useful for applying vim-macro-like edits to multiple files. She walks through how she translated a simple Vim workflow into an ed script to insert lines after a pattern match. The post reframes ed from a terrifying relic into an occasionally useful tool for batch file editing.
key_points: Any ed editing session corresponds to a script that can be replayed, making it a practical tool for batch file editing
Ed commands map fairly directly to Vim commands, so translating a Vim macro into an ed script is straightforward
The name 'grep' originates from ed syntax (g/re/p), which is a fun piece of Unix history
Ed's default error message is just '?' with no explanation, which makes it intimidating but can be configured to give better messages
money_quotes: Basically if you do something wrong, ed will just print out a single, unhelpful, ?.
I'd basically dismissed ed as an old arcane Unix tool that had no practical use today.
The other neat thing about Ed is that any Ed session corresponds to a script that you can replay!
So if I know Ed, then I can use Ed basically as a way to easily apply vim-macro-like programs to my files.
It was super surprising and delightful to me to find a practical use for Ed!
The most compelling thing about Ed is that I use simple Vim macros a lot, and it's a pretty direct way to translate a Vim macro into a way to batch edit a bunch of files.
key_insight: The ed text editor, often dismissed as an obsolete Unix relic, becomes genuinely useful when you realize that any ed session is a replayable script — making it a simple way to batch-apply Vim-macro-like edits across many files.
